signal.title: LEE BAE – EN ATTENDANT: WAITING
signal.body:
## LEE BAE – EN ATTENDANT: WAITING

### MUSEUM SAN, WONJU, SOUTH KOREA

### APR 7 – DEC 13, 2026

Known for his charcoal-based practice, Lee Bae presents his solo exhibition En attendant: Waiting at Museum SAN in Wonju. The exhibition surveys more than three decades of the artist’s work across painting, sculpture, installation, and video, centered on charcoal as his defining medium.

Throughout his work, charcoal functions not only as a medium but as a conceptual framework. It embodies cycles of creation, extinction, and transformation—processes inherent to its making, where wood is burned, reduced, and reconstituted into a new material. These cycles extend into broader considerations of nature, time, and existence, which remain consistent themes across Lee’s practice.

At Museum SAN, the exhibition unfolds as a continuous spatial journey that integrates architecture, landscape, and artwork. The presentation spans both indoor galleries and outdoor sculpture fields, reflecting the institution’s founding concept of “Space, Art, Nature.” Visitors move from the museum’s entrance—marked by the monumental charcoal work Issu du feu (From Fire)—through a sequence of interior spaces, including the lobby installation of sixteen Brushstroke paintings and the contrasting “White” and “Black” gallery environments, before arriving at Becoming, a large-scale installation combining projected brushwork and soil from the artist’s hometown of Cheongdo.

The title En attendant (“Waiting”) reflects an active condition rather than a passive state. For Lee Bae, waiting is understood as a period of incompletion and transformation—an essential phase within both artistic process and natural cycles. This concept resonates throughout the exhibition, where material, space, and time converge to create an evolving and immersive environment.

signal.title: CENTRE POMPIDOU OPENING THIS JUNE
signal.body:
Centre Pompidou Seoul is set to open in June 2026, marking a landmark moment for Seoul’s contemporary art landscape. The institution brings one of the world’s most prestigious museums to the city, providing a platform for global modern and contemporary art while fostering cultural exchange between Korea, France, and the wider international art community.

---

## PARTNERSHIP & SPACE

Centre Pompidou Seoul, established in partnership with the Hanwha Foundation, operates under a four-year agreement, with collaborative programming designed to present two major exhibitions per year. The museum is housed in the iconic 63 Building in Yeouido, Seoul’s leading financial and cultural district. The renovated space spans approximately 3,300 m² (1,000 pyeong), featuring two large exhibition galleries flooded with natural light during the day and illuminated to the cityscape at night—a concept described as the “box of light.” The architectural redesign was led by French architect Jean-Michel Wilmotte, whose portfolio includes renovations for the Louvre Museum, the Élysée Palace, and Incheon International Airport.

---

## INAUGURAL EXHIBITION – THE CUBISTS: INVENTING MODERN VISION

The opening exhibition, The Cubists: Inventing Modern Vision, highlights Cubism as a pivotal movement in 20th-century art. Beyond serving as a standard survey of the Pompidou’s collection, the exhibition offers a rigorous art-historical framework, tracing Cubism’s inception, dissemination, and global development through a chronological narrative. Curated collaboratively by French and Korean teams, the exhibition situates Cubism as a lens through which modern visuality emerged, signaling a symbolic new beginning for the museum in Seoul.

Highlights of the exhibition include Picasso’s monumental ballet stage curtain, Rideau pour le ballet “Mercure” (1924), presented in Korea for the first time, alongside 90 works—including paintings and sculptures—by more than 40 artists, such as Pablo Picasso, Wassily Kandinsky, Henri Matisse, Marc Chagall, Fernand Léger, and Juan Gris. The exhibition is organized into eight thematic sections, offering audiences unprecedented access to works rarely seen in Korea. A special segment, “Korea Focus,” explores the intersections of Western Cubism with early 20th-century Korean modern art, photography, literature, and dance.

---

Pablo Picasso, "Violin" (1914), ©Centre Pompidou

## UPCOMING PROGRAM HIGHLIGHTS

Over the next four years, Centre Pompidou Seoul will present two major exhibitions annually, drawing primarily from the Pompidou’s renowned collections in Paris. Planned exhibitions include:

- Solo and thematic exhibitions on Marc Chagall, Wassily Kandinsky, Henri Matisse and the Fauves.

- Exhibitions emphasizing Surrealism and abstract art.

- Major exhibitions highlighting women artists, long underrepresented in art history.

- A large-scale exhibition on Constantin Brâncuși, the first of its kind in South Korea.

In parallel, the museum is part of a broader ecosystem of artist support initiatives, including the Hanwha Foundation’s Youngmin International Residency Program and the New York-based Space ZeroOne. These programs aim to support emerging artists, foster experimentation, and expand global artistic exchange.

---

Centre Pompidou Seoul aspires to serve as a dynamic hub for contemporary art in Asia, bridging Korea with France and the global art scene, and offering visitors a new cultural experience that integrates international modern art with Seoul’s vibrant artistic community.

Beyond presenting a world-class collection, Centre Pompidou Seoul signals a turning point for Korea’s contemporary art landscape. By hosting one of the most prestigious global museums, the city positions itself as a serious player on the international art stage, fostering cross-cultural dialogue and inspiring new modes of artistic experimentation. For KALDI, sharing this development on our platform highlights not just the exhibition, but the evolving infrastructure and ambition of Seoul’s art ecosystem—inviting our audience to understand how global institutions are shaping the future of art in Asia.

signal.title: ART BASEL HONG KONG 2026
signal.body:
Image courtesy of Art Basel

Art Basel Hong Kong 2026 closed on March 29 after five days at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre. With 240 galleries from 41 countries and 91,500 visitors, the fair delivered steady sales across all price points — from entry-level works under $5,000 to transactions exceeding $3.8 million.

---

## I. MARKET TONE: STRONG DEMAND, MEASURED PACE

The defining word of this edition was measured. Fair filled quickly on VIP Preview day, but the rhythm was markedly different from pre-pandemic fairs. Collectors spent time in conversation, researched before committing, and prioritized relationships with galleries over first-day impulse buys.

Yet commercial results were solid. Transactions were reported from sub-$5K digital editions to $3.8M paintings, and galleries consistently noted the presence of new, younger collectors entering the market. David Zwirner called it "a new generation entering the Asian art market." White Cube reported approximately £5 million in total sales with around 20 works placed. Perrotin sold roughly 70% of its booth on the first day.

This fair was less about recovery and more about recalibration. The blue-chip market has consolidated around fewer but more committed buyers. The breadth of mid-range and entry-level transactions signals genuine market health rather than top-heavy anxiety. The structural shift from FOMO-driven speculation to legacy-minded collecting is underway.

Image courtesy of Art Basel

---

## II. TOP SALES — VERIFIED TRANSACTIONS

Image courtesy of Art Basel

### SEVEN-FIGURE SALES ($1M+)

### HIGH SIX-FIGURE SALES ($500K–$1M)

### NOTABLE MID-RANGE ($100K–$500K, SELECTED)

Jade Fadojutimi ~$465K (Taka Ishii) · Lynne Drexler $425K (Berry Campbell) · Andra Ursuța $400K (David Zwirner) · Mamma Andersson $400K (David Zwirner) · Qiu Xiaofei $395K (Hauser & Wirth, Asian foundation) · Megan Rooney ~$372K (Thaddaeus Ropac) · Flora Yukhnovich $325K (Hauser & Wirth) · Howardena Pindell $325K (White Cube) · Huma Bhabha $300K (David Zwirner) · Yan Pei-Ming €250K–€350K (MASSIMODECARLO) · Alice Baber $275K (Berry Campbell) · Anne Imhof ~$253K (Sprüth Magers, Asian institution) · Eddie Martinez $250K (Leeahn Gallery) · Lucas Arruda $250K (David Zwirner) · Etel Adnan $180K (White Cube) · Judy Chicago $165K (Jessica Silverman) · Rirkrit Tiravanija $160K each (Gladstone) · Isamu Noguchi $150K (White Cube) · Marina Rheingantz $150K (White Cube) · Salvo $135K (Sprüth Magers) · Atsushi Kaga $125K (Jessica Silverman) · Elaine de Kooning $100K (Berry Campbell) · Matthew Barney $100K (Gladstone).

---

Lee Shin Ja's work at Tina Kim Gallery, Image courtesy of Art Basel

## III. KOREAN ARTISTS — CONFIRMED SALES

Demand for Korean artists was broad — spanning generations, media, and price points. Lee Bul was arguably the most talked-about Asian artist at the fair, buoyed by her concurrent retrospective at M+.

KALDI ART's Insight — Reading the Korean Market:

Lee Bul is the standout case. Since joining Hauser & Wirth, her price level has settled in the $260K–$275K range for new work, while early pieces at Lehmann Maupin traded at $200K–$300K. The M+ retrospective directly drove market demand — a textbook example of how strategic alignment between institutional exposure and fair timing amplifies both visibility and value. This is the playbook.

The Dansaekhwa generation (Park Seo-Bo, Ha Chong-Hyun, Lee Bae, Kim Taek Sang) showed continued demand through Johyun Gallery's 37-work haul. Ha Chong-Hyun's Kukje price ($180K) becomes a reference point for measuring the gap against secondary auction results — the kind of primary-to-secondary spread that defines advisory opportunity.

Lee ShinJa ($150K–$200K to an Asian museum) and Kim Yun Shin ($100K–$150K, museum + European collector) confirm an ongoing institutional reassessment of Korean women artists. Both sales coincided with active museum retrospectives — the same pattern as Lee Bul.

Lee Mokha at $85K (Jason Haam, sold on opening day) is notable as a price-formation moment for an emerging Korean artist on the international stage.

Moka Lee, Innuendo 02 (Here and Now), 2026, Oil on cotton, 194 x 157.5 cm @courtesy of Jason Haam and the Artist

---

## IV. INSTITUTIONAL ACQUISITIONS

Institutional buying was a defining feature. Confirmed placements include:

- Lee Bul → Asian private museum (Hauser & Wirth)

- Louise Bourgeois Couple → Asian foundation (Hauser & Wirth)

- Martha Jungwirth → Chinese institution (Thaddaeus Ropac)

- Anne Imhof → Asian institution (Sprüth Magers)

- Lee ShinJa → Asian museum (Tina Kim Gallery)

- Kim Yun Shin → Asian museum (Lehmann Maupin)

- Qiu Xiaofei → Asian foundation (Hauser & Wirth)

- Dinh Q. Lê → Asian foundation (P·P·O·W)

Representatives from over 170 museums and foundations across 27 countries attended, including Centre Pompidou, Tate, MoMA, the Metropolitan Museum of Art, the Guggenheim, SFMOMA, Fondation Beyeler, Louvre Abu Dhabi, M+, and the Leeum Samsung Museum of Art.

---

## V. WHAT DIDN'T SELL — THE QUIET SIGNAL

Not everything moved. Pace brought what was understood to be the most expensive work at the fair — an €11.5M Modigliani — which did not sell during the fair's run. The upper floor of the fair was noticeably slower than the ground floor, where blue-chip galleries were concentrated. Several gallerists on the second floor described a measured but not frantic pace. Pearl Lam Galleries' director Charmaine Chan noted that "this year, sales are a bit slower" and that Hong Kong's market "is still a question mark."

These signals matter. A fair where everything sells indiscriminately is not a healthy market — it's a bubble. The fact that collectors were selective, that some high-priced lots didn't move, and that buying spread across price points rather than clustering at the top, suggests a market that is functioning with discipline rather than euphoria.

---

A work by Amedeo Modigliani presented by Pace Gallery

---

## VI. DIGITAL ART — ZERO 10 ASIA DEBUT

Zero 10, Art Basel's global initiative for digital-era art, made its Asian debut. Reported sales include ThankYouX $50K (Plan X), Sougwen Chung $65K each (Fellowship x ARTXCODE), Qu Leilei $45K and Tim Yip $35K (Asprey Studio), DeeKay editions at 6 ETH (~$13K) each (AOTM), and Emi Kusano (√K Contemporary), which sold immediately on opening.

ZERO 10 EXHIBITORS AT ART BASEL HONG KONG 2026

---

## VII. PARALLEL MOMENTUM: THE AUCTION CIRCUIT

The commercial energy of Art Basel Hong Kong was mirrored—and in some cases, amplified—by the concurrent spring auctions. The performance of the major houses, particularly Christie’s, served as a quantitative "stress test" for the Asian market’s recovery.

1. Christie’s Hong Kong: The "White Glove" Signal

Christie’s 20th/21st Century Art Evening Sale on March 27 at The Henderson was a watershed moment, achieving a rare 100% sell-through rate (White Glove Sale).

- Total Revenue: HK$655.7M (~$83.8M), a 17% increase compared to the March 2024 evening sale.

- Market Health Indicator: The hammer price reached 117% of the combined low estimate. This indicates that buyers were not just looking for deals, but were actively "chasing" works above their base valuations—a key sign of returning market confidence.

- Combined Results: Across the Evening and Day sales, Christie’s sold 94% of all lots, totaling approximately $114 million.

2. Top Auction Performers

- Gerhard Richter: The 1991 masterpiece Abstraktes Bild led the week, selling for $11.8M. As one of the largest works in its series, its successful placement underscored the demand for top-tier European Blue-chip material in Asia.

- Sanyu: Cheval agenouillé sur un tapis (1950s–60s), appearing on the market for the first time in 50 years from the prestigious Lévy family collection, sparked a bidding war that ended at $8.2M.

- Old Masters Expansion: For the first time, Christie’s included Old Masters in the Hong Kong Evening Sale. Johannes Goedaert’s 17th-century still life sold for $1.3M, doubling its low estimate. This suggests a strategic diversification of Asian portfolios into Western historical categories.

3. Korean Artists in the Secondary Market

The auction results provided a vital "reality check" for the primary sales seen at the fair. Consistent with the fair's results, Korean masters showed steady liquidity:

- Lee Ufan: From Line (1978) — $1.27M

- Rhee Seund-ja: Une anémone sauvage (1962) — $730,250

- Lee Bae: Brushstroke-9F (2023) — $308,329

---

## VIII. CONCLUSION: RISK MANAGEMENT AND THE EVOLUTION OF COLLECTING

The convergence of Art Basel and the major auctions confirms that the 2026 market has entered a phase of "Measured Vitality." While we are not seeing the frantic, speculative boom of previous cycles, the recovery is grounded in structural discipline.

The shift is twofold:

1. From Investment to Experience: Art is increasingly consumed as an "experiential good" rather than a purely speculative asset. Collectors are prioritizing provenance, historical significance, and the long-term legacy of the artists.

2. Strategic Resilience: Hong Kong maintains its status as the world’s third-largest auction hub (14.5% global share in 2025). The successful integration of Old Masters and the continued dominance of Korean and Japanese artists signal a market that is broadening its base to mitigate risk.

---

This report is based exclusively on cross-verified data from Art Basel's official statements, Artsy, ARTnews, Artnet News, The Art Newspaper, and Observer.  Fair prices are based on galleries' voluntary disclosures and may differ from final transaction terms. Approximate prices are noted accordingly.

I KR I

아시아 최대 아트페어 아트바젤 홍콩 2026이 3월 29일 막을 내렸다. 41개국 240개 갤러리가 참가한 이번 에디션은 총 91,500명의 방문객을 기록하며, VIP 프리뷰부터 일반 공개일까지 전 가격대에 걸친 안정적 판매가 이어졌다.

---

## I. 시장 톤: "강한 수요, 신중한 접근"

올해 페어의 핵심 키워드는 measured pace였다. 페어 현장은 VIP 프리뷰 데이때 부터 북적였지만, 컬렉터들은 첫날 충동구매보다는 갤러리와의 대화와 리서치를 우선했다. 이는 팬데믹 이전의 투기적 분위기와는 분명히 다른 리듬이다.

그럼에도 상업적 성과는 견실했다. 엔트리 레벨($5K 이하)부터 $3.8M까지 전 가격대에서 거래가 보고되었고, 특히 아시아-태평양 지역 컬렉터와 기관의 존재감이 두드러졌다. 젊은 컬렉터의 유입이 다수 갤러리에 의해 언급되었으며, David Zwirner는 공식 코멘트에서 이를 "새로운 세대의 아시아 미술시장 진입"으로 표현했다.

이번 페어는 '회복'이라기보다 '재편'에 가깝다. 소수의 확실한 구매자 중심으로 블루칩 시장이 공고해졌고, 중간·입문 가격대의 폭넓은 거래는 시장 건강성의 지표다. 2019년 이전의 FOMO 기반 구매가 유산(legacy) 기반 컬렉팅으로 전환되는 과정이며, 이 구조적 변화는 계속해서 지켜볼 만하다.

---

## II. TOP SALES — 확인된 주요 거래

### $1M 이상 (7-FIGURE)

### $500K – $1M

### 주요 MID-RANGE ($100K – $500K, 선별)

Jade Fadojutimi ~$465K (Taka Ishii), Lynne Drexler $425K (Berry Campbell), Andra Ursuța $400K (David Zwirner), Qiu Xiaofei $395K (Hauser & Wirth, 아시아 재단), Megan Rooney ~$372K (Thaddaeus Ropac), Flora Yukhnovich $325K (Hauser & Wirth), Howardena Pindell $325K (White Cube), Huma Bhabha $300K (David Zwirner), Yan Pei-Ming €250K–€350K (MASSIMODECARLO), Alice Baber $275K (Berry Campbell), Anne Imhof ~$253K (Sprüth Magers, 아시아 기관), Lucas Arruda $250K (David Zwirner), Eddie Martinez $250K (Leeahn Gallery), Judy Chicago $165K (Jessica Silverman), Rirkrit Tiravanija $160K each (Gladstone), Atsushi Kaga $125K (Jessica Silverman).

---

## III. 한국 작가 — 확인된 판매

한국 작가에 대한 수요는 세대와 매체를 가리지 않고 폭넓게 나타났다. 특히 이불은 M+ 회고전과의 시너지로 페어 전체에서 가장 주목받은 아시아 작가 중 하나였다.

KALDI ART 인사이트 — 한국 작가 시장 읽기:

이불은 이번 페어에서 가장 주목할 한국 작가 케이스다. Hauser & Wirth 이적 이후 가격 레벨이 $260K–$275K대로 안착했고, Lehmann Maupin에서도 초기작이 $200K–$300K에 거래되었다. M+ 회고전이라는 기관 노출이 시장 수요를 직접적으로 견인한 사례로, 기관전과 페어 타이밍의 전략적 연동이 얼마나 중요한지를 보여준다.

단색화 세대(박서보, 하종현, 이배, 김택상)는 조현화랑이 37점을 판매하며 여전한 수요를 확인했다. 하종현의 국제갤러리 가격($180K)은 옥션 2차 시장과의 괴리를 측정하는 기준점이 된다.

이신자의 Tina Kim Gallery 판매($150K–$200K, 아시아 뮤지엄)는 한국 여성 작가의 기관급 재평가가 진행 중임을 보여주며, 김윤신의 Lehmann Maupin 판매도 같은 맥락이다. 호암미술관 회고전과의 타이밍 역시 이불과 동일한 패턴이다.

이목하($85K, 제이슨함)의 첫날 판매는 한국 신진 작가의 국제 가격 형성 사례로 주목할 만하다.

---

## IV. 기관 매입 — 확인된 건

기관 구매는 이번 페어의 주요 특징 중 하나다.

- 이불 → 아시아 사립미술관 (Hauser & Wirth)

- Louise Bourgeois Couple → 아시아 재단 (Hauser & Wirth)

- Martha Jungwirth → 중국 기관 (Thaddaeus Ropac)

- Anne Imhof → 아시아 기관 (Sprüth Magers)

- 이신자 → 아시아 뮤지엄 (Tina Kim)

- 김윤신 → 아시아 뮤지엄 (Lehmann Maupin)

- Qiu Xiaofei → 아시아 재단 (Hauser & Wirth)

- Dinh Q. Lê → 아시아 재단 (P·P·O·W)

170개 이상 뮤지엄·재단에서 27개국 대표가 참석했으며, Centre Pompidou, Tate, MoMA, Met, Guggenheim, SFMOMA, Fondation Beyeler, Louvre Abu Dhabi, M+, 리움삼성미술관 등이 포함된다.

---

## V. 미판매 작품이 보내는 신호: 시장의 침착한 이면

이번 페어에서 모든 작품이 주인을 찾은 것은 아니다. 페이스(Pace) 갤러리가 출품한 이번 페어 최고가 작품인 1,150만 유로(약 167억 원) 상당의 모딜리아니(Modigliani)는 페어 기간 내에 판매되지 않았다. 또한 블루칩 갤러리들이 밀집한 1층에 비해 2층 전시장은 상대적으로 흐름이 느린 편이었다. 2층의 여러 갤러리 관계자들은 시장의 속도가 신중하며 결코 급하지 않다고 설명했다. 펄 람 갤러리(Pearl Lam Galleries)의 디렉터 샤메인 찬(Charmaine Chan)은 "올해 판매는 다소 느린 편"이라며 홍콩 시장이 "여전히 물음표 상태"라고 언급하기도 했다.

이러한 신호들은 시장 분석에 있어 매우 중요하다. 모든 작품이 무분별하게 팔려나가는 시장은 건강한 상태가 아니라 오히려 '거품'에 가깝기 때문이다. 컬렉터들이 선택적으로 움직이고, 일부 고가 작품이 유찰되었으며, 구매 수요가 상단에만 쏠리지 않고 다양한 가격대로 분산되었다는 사실은 현재 미술 시장이 광풍(Euphoria)이 아닌 **'규율(Discipline)'**에 의해 작동하고 있음을 시사한다.

결국 2026년의 홍콩은 단순한 경기 회복을 넘어, 시장이 지능적으로 재구조화되고 있음을 보여주는 가장 명확한 사례다.

---

## VI. 디지털 아트 — ZERO 10 아시아 데뷔

Zero 10 섹터가 아시아에 처음 도입되었다. ThankYouX $50K (Plan X), Sougwen Chung $65K each (Fellowship), Qu Leilei $45K (Asprey Studio), DeeKay 6 ETH ~$13K each (AOTM) 등 실거래가 보고되었으며, √K Contemporary의 Emi Kusano 작품은 개막 즉시 판매되었다.

---

## VII. 마켓의 이중주: 경매 시장의 동반 상승세

아트바젤 홍콩의 상업적 열기는 동시기에 개최된 주요 경매 결과로 더욱 공고해졌다. 특히 크리스티(Christie’s)를 필두로 한 주요 경매사들의 실적은 아시아 미술 시장의 회복 탄력성을 측정하는 정량적인 '스트레스 테스트' 역할을 했다.

### 1. 크리스티 홍콩: '화이트 글로브'가 보낸 강력한 신호

지난 3월 27일, 홍콩 더 핸더슨(The Henderson) 빌딩에서 열린 크리스티 '20/21세기 미술 이브닝 경매'는 시장의 우려를 불식시키는 기념비적인 결과를 냈다.

- 100% 낙찰률 기록: 출품작 37점이 모두 낙찰되는 ‘화이트 글로브(White Glove)’ 세일을 달성했다.

- 매출 규모: 총 6억 5,570만 홍콩달러(약 1,263억 원)를 기록, 이는 전년 동기 대비 17% 증가한 수치다.

- 시장의 건강성: 최종 낙찰 총액이 최저 예상가 합계의 117%를 기록했다. 이는 컬렉터들이 단순히 저가 매수를 노린 것이 아니라, 가치 있는 작품을 확보하기 위해 최저가를 넘어 적극적인 경합을 벌였음을 시사한다.

### 2. 주요 경매 하이라이트

- 게르하르트 리히터(Gerhard Richter): 1991년작 <Abstraktes Bild>가 약 1,180만 달러(약 150억 원)에 낙찰되며 이번 시즌 최고가를 기록했다. 이는 아시아 시장 내 서구 블루칩 마스터피스에 대한 여전한 수요를 증명한다.

- 사뉴(Sanyu): 약 50년 만에 시장에 나온 <Cheval agenouillé sur un tapis>는 치열한 전화 경합 끝에 820만 달러(약 105억 원)에 낙찰되었다.

- 올드 마스터의 확장: 홍콩 이브닝 세일 최초로 포함된 17세기 네덜란드 화가 요하네스 구다트(Johannes Goedaert)의 정물화가 예상가의 두 배인 130만 달러에 낙찰되었다. 아시아 컬렉터들의 포트폴리오가 서구 고전 미술까지 다각화되고 있음을 보여주는 전략적 지표이다.

### 3. 한국 작가의 2차 시장 성적

페어에서의 1차 판매 열기는 경매라는 2차 시장에서도 고르게 나타났다.

- 이우환: <From Line> (1978) — 127만 달러 (약 16.3억 원)

- 이성자: <Une anémone sauvage> (1962) — 73만 달러 (약 9.3억 원)

- 이배: <Brushstroke-9F> (2023) — 30만 8천 달러 (약 4억 원)

---

## VIII. 마치며: 리스크 관리와 컬렉팅의 진화

2026년 상반기 홍콩 마켓은 '신중한 활력(Measured Vitality)'의 단계로 진입했다. 과거와 같은 투기적 붐은 사라졌으나, 시장의 체질은 훨씬 견고해졌다.

핵심적인 시장 변화:

1. 경험재로서의 미술 소비: 컬렉터들은 더 이상 미술품을 단순한 투자 자산으로만 보지 않는다. 소장 이력(Provenance), 역사적 맥락, 그리고 작가의 장기적 비전을 중시하는 '경험재' 중심의 소비가 주류를 이루고 있다.

2. 전략적 회복세: 홍콩은 2025년 글로벌 경매 점유율 14.5%를 유지하며 세계 3위 허브로서의 지위를 공고히 했다. 올드 마스터의 성공적인 도입과 한국·일본 작가들의 견조한 성장은 시장이 리스크 분산을 위해 그 저변을 넓히고 있음을 의미한다.

KALDI ART 총평: 크리스티의 화이트 글로브 세일과 아트바젤의 수백만 달러대 거래는 아시아 시장이 '불확실성'의 터널을 지나 '질적 재편'의 시대로 들어섰음을 나타낸다. 특히 한국 미술의 경우, 이불(Lee Bul)과 김윤신처럼 기관의 재조명과 상업적 성과가 맞물리는 '전략적 정렬'이 시장의 성숙도를 입증한다. 이제 시장은 단순한 '포모(FOMO)'에서 벗어나, 안목 있는 레거시 컬렉터(Legacy Collector)들에 의해 움직이고 있다.

---

본 리포트는 Art Basel 공식발표, Artsy, ARTnews, Artnet News, The Art Newspaper, Observer를 기반으로 작성한 시장 분석이다. 가격이 "Approx" 또는 범위값으로 표시된 경우 갤러리 보고 기준이며, 최종 거래 조건과 다를 수 있음.

signal.title: MARCH ON VIEW
signal.body:
From institutional retrospectives to gallery debuts, from postwar masters to living voices reshaping the present, March 2026 offers one of the densest exhibition calendars in recent memory. London, New York, Florence, Berlin, Amsterdam, Warsaw, Paris. Here is what we are watching.

National Portrait Gallery, London

#### LUCIAN FREUD: DRAWING INTO PAINTING

Feb 12 – May 4

The first major exhibition dedicated to Lucian Freud's drawing practice. Over 100 works reveal the connection between his precise draftsmanship and his commanding portraits.

Stedelijk Museum, Amsterdam

#### DANH VO: ΠΝΕΥ͂ΜΑ (ἜΛΙΣΣΑ)

Feb 14 – Aug 2

The most comprehensive European survey to date of Danh Vo. Spanning sculpture, found objects, and installations, the exhibition explores history, intimacy, and the lingering traces of empire.

Tate Modern, London

#### TRACEY EMIN: A SECOND LIFE

Feb 27 – Aug 31

A landmark exhibition spanning over 40 years of work. Raw, intimate, and unapologetically honest, Emin's practice confronts themes of love, loss, trauma, and the body.

Royal Academy of Arts, London

#### ROSE WYLIE: THE PICTURE COMES FIRST

Feb 28 – Apr 19

The first British female artist to occupy all of the Royal Academy's main galleries. Over 90 works inspired by cinema, celebrity culture, the Blitz, and Snow White.

Whitney Museum, New York

#### WHITNEY BIENNIAL 2026

Mar 8 – Aug 23

The 82nd edition, featuring 56 artists, curated by Marcela Guerrero and Drew Sawyer. Mood over manifesto.

Serpentine North, London

#### DAVID HOCKNEY: A YEAR IN NORMANDIE

Mar 12 – Aug 23

A monumental 90-metre iPad frieze, capturing the shifting seasons of his Normandy studio in one continuous, immersive landscape.

Palazzo Strozzi, Florence

#### ROTHKO IN FLORENCE

Mar 14 – Aug 23

Over 70 works curated by Christopher Rothko, extending into the Museo di San Marco and Michelangelo's Biblioteca Laurenziana. American abstraction meets the Renaissance that inspired it.

David Zwirner, New York

### ELIZABETH PEYTON: MOUNTAINS IN MY HEART

Mar 19 – May 2

Peyton's first New York solo with David Zwirner. New paintings and works on paper extending her intimate portrait practice.

Neue Nationalgalerie, Berlin

#### BRANCUSI

Mar 20 – Aug 9

Over 150 works. His first major exhibition in Germany in more than 50 years, featuring a partial reconstruction of his Paris studio — shown outside France for the first time since 1957.

Museum of Modern Art, Warsaw

#### JULIE MEHRETU: KAIROS / HAUNTOLOGICAL VARIATIONS

Mar 20 – Aug 30

A major solo survey in Warsaw's new museum. Layered abstractions exploring geopolitical rupture, migration, and the weight of history.

Almine Rech, Paris

#### FORMING THE MONOCHROME: MASTERS OF DANSAEKHWA

Mar 21 – May 23

Ha Chong-Hyun, Lee Ufan, Park Seo-Bo, Yun Hyong-Keun, and Chung Sang-Hwa. Key figures of Dansaekhwa in Paris, marking the 140th anniversary of diplomatic relations between France and Korea.

Eleven exhibitions across seven cities. What connects them is not geography or generation, but a shared sense of timeliness — each show arriving at a moment when its subject demands renewed attention. The season is open.

signal.title: TEFAF 2026
signal.body:
@Courtesy of TEFAF

TEFAF Maastricht has always been defined by its breadth: 7,000 years of art history, nearly 300 dealers, a vetting process unmatched in the industry. What the 39th edition revealed is that breadth is no longer just a logistical achievement — it has become a curatorial philosophy. The fair's most significant contemporary presentations this year were built not on isolation but on dialogue: with history, with other mediums, with the weight of context that only TEFAF can provide.

That context was not without friction. New EU cultural property import regulations, in full force since June 2025, created considerable logistical strain — particularly for non-European works — concentrating the fair's character in a more European register than in previous years. Geopolitical disruption further thinned collector attendance from the Middle East and parts of Asia. The result was a more selective and deliberate.

### THE KEY TRENDS OF THE YEAR

The Old/New Crossover as Standard Practice

Across the contemporary section, the most persuasive booths were structured around historical dialogue. Emerging painters shown alongside pre-Surrealists; Modernist canvases in conversation with living sculptors; photography placed against Enlightenment cartography. What was once a novel curatorial gesture at TEFAF has become the expected grammar — and the galleries that have mastered it are showing the most confidence.

Photography Elevated to Primary Medium

The most structurally significant change at this edition was photography's transition from supporting role to independent category — a shift Will Korner, TEFAF's Head of Fairs, explicitly signaled in advance. In the Focus section, Berlin's Galerie Thomas Schulte presented a dedicated solo presentation of Robert Mapplethorpe's flower portraits, whose resonance with Dutch cultural identity and the Old Master still-life tradition gave the work an unexpectedly native logic within these walls. Separately, Michael Hoppen Gallery and Daniel Crouch Rare Books staged Cities, like dreams — pairing Sohei Nishino's monumental photographic diorama maps with 18th-century town plans from Crouch's collection, including the world premiere of Diorama Map Venice, assembled from over 450 rolls of film. These were not supplementary displays. They were programmatic arguments about where photography belongs in the canon.

The Rise of Female Artists

The visibility of women artists this year was not concentrated in a single booth or curatorial gesture — it was distributed across the section as a whole. From Tracey Emin and Howardena Pindell at White Cube, to Eileen Agar, Sheila Hicks, Pacita Abad, Ana Mendieta, and Dorothea Tanning at Alison Jacques, to Helen Frankenthaler prints at Lyndsey Ingram. The pattern reflects a sustained shift in how galleries are constructing their fair programs — not as correction, but as priority.

### BOOTH HIGHLIGHTS&SALES

Thalita Hamaoui, Estrela, materia, memoria, 2026  @COURTESY MARIANNE BOESKY

Marianne Boesky — Thalita Hamaoui and Odilon Redon

Marianne Boesky placed Thalita Hamaoui's lush, surreally layered landscape paintings alongside works by pre-Surrealist Odilon Redon — two artists separated by more than a century, united by chromatic intensity and a shared fascination with nature as psychological territory. The arrangement was quietly persuasive, and the market responded in kind: eleven of twelve works sold on opening day, at prices between $16,000 and $60,000. That an emerging contemporary painter generated this velocity in a context historically anchored in Old Masters is not incidental. It speaks to the sophistication of the framing as much as the quality of the work.

Kamel Mennour — Vallotton, Giacometti, Rondinone

Mennour constructed his booth around the idea of formal and emotional continuity across time. Félix Vallotton's Liseuse (1912) — a woman reading, her curves deliberately echoed by the adjacent Camille Henrot bronze — anchored a presentation that moved through Giacometti and Ugo Rondinone with consistent internal logic. By day two: the Vallotton to an institution at €350,000; the Giacometti to a private collector at €700,000; the Rondinone at €350,000. Combined, approximately €1.4 million. The commercial result was strong; the curatorial argument was stronger.

Caroline Walker, Showbar, 2026 @Courtesy of GRIMM

GRIMM — Caroline Walker, Showbar (2026)

GRIMM centred its booth on a large-scale painting made specifically for this edition by Scottish artist Caroline Walker. Showbar (2026), priced at £175,000, extends her Holiday Park series — developed through periods spent at family resorts in 2023 and 2024, photographing women at work. Walker's core concerns — labor, visibility, the spaces women occupy and are defined by — do not diminish within TEFAF's historically weighted context. If anything, the context sharpens them. The booth also included works by Angela Heisch and Tjebbe Beekman, sustaining a coherent curatorial proposition across three distinct practices.

Galleria Continua — The Broadest Spectrum on the Floor

Among the contemporary presentations this year, Galleria Continua assembled the widest range of established voices in a single space: Ai Weiwei, Anish Kapoor, Hiroshi Sugimoto, Michelangelo Pistoletto, and Berlinde De Bruyckere, whose wax and mirror works extended her 2024 Venice Biennale presentation. The booth embodied the gallery's long-held conviction that institutional-scale names can coexist coherently — and that within a fair defined by historical depth, contemporary art is most powerful when positioned inside history, not against it.

### CLOSING

TEFAF 2026 made one thing clear: historical context does not dilute contemporary work — it amplifies it. The booths that resonated most were not those presenting the present in isolation, but those that made the conversation between past and present feel unresolved, and therefore alive. For collectors and advisors who operate at this intersection, the fair remains without equal.

### 동시대 미술과 역사적 맥락이 교차하는곳, TEFAF 마스트리흐트 2026 하이라이트

TEFAF 마스트리히트는 언제나 ‘범위’로 정의되어 왔다. 7,000년에 걸친 미술사, 거의 300개의 갤러리, 그리고 업계에서 비교할 수 없는 심사 과정까지. 39회째를 맞이한 이번 에디션이 보여준 것은, 단순한 규모의 달성이 아니라 ‘큐레이션 철학’으로서의 폭이었다. 올해 페어에서 가장 주목할 만한 현대 미술 전시는 고립된 작품이 아닌 ‘대화’를 중심으로 구성되었다. 역사와, 다른 매체와, 오직 TEFAF만이 제공할 수 있는 맥락의 무게와의 대화였다.

물론 이러한 맥락이 늘 순조로운 것은 아니었다. 2025년 6월 발효된 EU 문화재 수입 규정은 상당한 물류적 부담을 초래했으며, 특히 비유럽권 작품의 참가에 영향을 미쳤다. 덕분에 이번 페어는 이전보다 더 유럽 중심적 성격을 띠게 되었다. 지정학적 혼란은 중동과 일부 아시아 지역 컬렉터들의 방문을 줄였고, 결과적으로 페어는 더 선택적이고 전략적인 성격을 띠게 되었다.

---

### 올해의 주요 트렌드

역사와 현대의 교차, 이제 표준이 되다
현대 섹션에서 가장 설득력 있는 부스들은 역사적 대화를 중심으로 구성되었다. 신진 화가의 작품이 초현실주의 이전 화가와 나란히 전시되거나, 모더니즘 캔버스가 살아 있는 조각가와 대화하고, 사진 작품이 계몽주의 지도와 맞닿는 등 과거와 현재의 연결을 보여주었다. TEFAF에서 한때 신선한 큐레이션적 시도로 여겨졌던 이러한 접근은 이제 표준적 ‘문법’이 되었으며, 이를 완벽히 구현한 갤러리들이 가장 자신감 있는 모습을 보여주었다.

사진, 주요 매체로 도약
이번 에디션에서 가장 구조적으로 중요한 변화는 사진이 기존의 보조적 역할에서 독립 카테고리로 자리 잡은 것이다. TEFAF의 페어 책임자 윌 코너(Will Korner)가 사전에 명확히 신호를 준 바 있다. 포커스 섹션에서 베를린의 갤러리 토마스 슐테(Galerie Thomas Schulte)는 로버트 메이플소프(Robert Mapplethorpe)의 꽃 초상 작품을 단독으로 전시하며, 네덜란드 문화 정체성과 올드 마스터 정물 전통과의 공명을 보여주었다. 마이클 호펜 갤러리(Michael Hoppen Gallery)와 다니엘 크라우치 희귀도서(Daniel Crouch Rare Books)는 “Cities, like dreams” 전시에서 니시노 소헤이(Sohei Nishino)의 거대한 사진 디오라마 지도와 18세기 도시 계획 도면을 나란히 배치하며, “Diorama Map Venice”를 세계 최초로 공개했다. 이들은 단순한 보조 전시가 아니라, 사진이 미술사에서 차지하는 위치에 대한 구조적 논증이었다.

여성 작가의 부상

올해 여성 작가의 가시성은 특정 부스나 큐레이션적 제스처에 국한되지 않았다. 화이트 큐브의 트레이시 에민(Tracey Emin)과 하워데나 핀델(Howardena Pindell), 앨리슨 자크(Alison Jacques)의 일린 아가(Eileen Agar), 실라 힉스(Sheila Hicks), 파시타 아바드(Pacita Abad), 아나 멘디에타(Ana Mendieta), 도로시아 탱(Dorothea Tanning), 린드지 잉그램(Lyndsey Ingram)의 헬렌 프랭켄탈러(Helen Frankenthaler) 프린트까지, 전체 섹션에 걸쳐 나타났다. 이는 갤러리들이 페어 프로그램을 구성하는 방식이 단순한 ‘수정’을 넘어선 ‘우선순위’의 변화를 반영한다.

---

### 부스 하이라이트 & 판매 실적

Marianne Boesky — Thalita Hamaoui와 Odilon Redon
마리안 보이스키는 탈리타 하마우이의 풍부하고 초현실적으로 겹쳐진 풍경화와 초현실주의 이전 화가 오딜롱 르동의 작품을 나란히 전시했다. 두 작가 사이에는 세기의 간격이 있지만, 색채의 강렬함과 자연을 심리적 영역으로 탐구하는 관심으로 연결된다. 결과는 조용하지만 설득력 있었고, 시장 역시 반응했다. 개막일에 12점 중 11점이 $16,000~$60,000에 판매됐다. 신진 현대 화가가 올드 마스터 중심의 맥락에서 이러한 속도를 보여준 것은 단순한 우연이 아니다. 작품의 질만큼이나 배치와 프레임의 정교함을 보여준다.

Kamel Mennour — Vallotton, Giacometti, Rondinone
메누르는 부스를 시간에 걸친 형식적·감정적 연속성을 중심으로 구성했다. 펠릭스 발로통(Félix Vallotton)의 <읽는 여인>(1912)과 인접한 카미유 에네로(Camille Henrot)의 청동 작품이 곡선을 반복하며, 지아코메티와 우고 론디노네를 잇는 논리를 제공했다. 이틀째에는 발로통 작품이 기관으로 €350,000, 지아코메티 작품이 개인 컬렉터로 €700,000, 론디노네 작품이 €350,000에 판매되며 총 약 €1.4백만의 성과를 냈다. 상업적 성과도 훌륭했지만, 큐레이션적 논증은 더 강력했다.

GRIMM — Caroline Walker, Showbar, 2026
그림 부스는 스코틀랜드 작가 캐롤라인 워커의 이번 에디션 전용 대형 작품 <Showbar (2026)>를 중심으로 구성됐다. £175,000에 책정된 이 작품은 2023~24년 가족 리조트에서 여성 노동을 촬영하며 발전시킨 Holiday Park 시리즈를 확장한다. 노동, 가시성, 여성의 공간과 정의 등 워커의 핵심 주제는 TEFAF의 역사적 맥락 속에서도 약화되지 않는다. 오히려 맥락이 이를 더욱 선명하게 한다. 부스에는 앤젤라 하이쉬(Angela Heisch)와 티에베 베크만(Tjebbe Beekman)의 작품도 함께 전시되며 세 가지 개별 작업 사이의 일관된 큐레이션적 제안을 보여주었다.

Galleria Continua — 가장 폭넓은 스펙트럼의 작가군 프레젠테이션
현대 미술 부문에서 갤러리아 콘티누아는 한 공간에서 가장 다양한 작가를 선보였다. 아이 웨이웨이(Ai Weiwei), 아니시 카푸어(Anish Kapoor), 히로시 스기모토(Hiroshi Sugimoto), 미켈란젤로 피스토렛토(Michelangelo Pistoletto), 베를린데 드 브뤼커레(Berlinde De Bruyckere) 등이 포함되며, 특히 드 브뤼커레의 왁스·거울 작품은 2024년 베니스 비엔날레 전시를 확장한다. 이 부스는 기관급 작가와의 공존이 가능하다는 오랜 갤러리 신념을 보여주며, 역사적 깊이로 정의되는 페어에서 현대 미술은 역사 안에 위치할 때 가장 강력함을 증명했다.

---

### 결론

TEFAF 2026이 보여준 한 가지 명확한 사실: 역사적 맥락은 현대 미술을 희석시키지 않는다. 오히려 그것을 증폭한다. 가장 강한 울림을 준 부스는 현재만을 고립시켜 보여준 곳이 아니라, 과거와 현재의 대화를 ‘미완성’으로 남겨두어 살아 있는 긴장을 만든 곳이었다.

signal.title: LA ART WEEK 2026: A CITY THAT SHOWED UP
signal.body:
## FRIEZE LOS ANGELES × FELIX ART FAIR

February 25 – March 1, 2026

Santa Monica Airport & Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel

KALDI Market Report

Frieze Los Angeles 2026. Image by Casey Kelbaugh/CKA. Courtesy of Frieze.

The numbers are in, the booths are down, and the crates are heading back to New York, London, Seoul, and São Paulo. LA Art Week 2026 — built around the seventh edition of Frieze Los Angeles and the eighth of Felix Art Fair — is over, and by nearly every available measure, it delivered.

More than 100 galleries from 24 countries presented at Frieze LA, welcoming over 32,000 visitors from 45 countries, with representatives from 160 museums and institutions attending across the week. From the first minutes of VIP preview on February 26 through the final public hours on March 1, dealers across both fairs reported not just sales, but something rarer: collective conviction.

That word matters more than it seems. This was a city still processing the aftermath of devastating wildfires that had torn through Altadena and the Pacific Palisades just over a year ago. The art world showed up in force for the 2025 edition too, but in a spirit closer to defiance. This year, the mood had shifted. "From the opening morning, it was clear that this year's edition marked a new level of confidence for Frieze Los Angeles," said Christine Messineo, Frieze's Director of Americas. "Collectors engaged with conviction across every section of the fair, and institutional participation was both deep and sustained."

#### THE SALES

The headline was a $2.8 million mixed-media work by Njideka Akunyili Crosby — sold by David Zwirner to a European foundation within the opening hours of VIP preview. It was the fair's single largest confirmed transaction, and it set the tone for what followed.

But the breadth across the fair was what stood out. This wasn't a single-gallery story.

White Cube sold three major sculptures from its solo Antony Gormley presentation, each priced between £500,000 and £800,000 — totaling well over £1.5 million. David Kordansky Gallery, one of LA's most respected local powerhouses, reported more than $2.4 million in sales, led by Jonas Wood's Bonsai #12 (2025) at $600,000. Pace Gallery cleared approximately $1.8 million, including a James Turrell installation at $950,000 — confirmed in the official closing statement — alongside a 1983 Jean Dubuffet painting at $475,000. Thaddaeus Ropac reported a Georg Baselitz painting at €1,000,000 and an Alex Katz 2022 canvas, Purple Split 3, at $700,000.

Almine Rech reported an Ewa Juszkiewicz painting in the $800,000–$850,000 range. Garth Greenan placed a Howardena Pindell at $875,000. Michael Rosenfeld Gallery confirmed more than $2 million from a group presentation. Hauser & Wirth — typically reticent on specifics — confirmed its entire solo presentation of newly represented painter Conny Maier sold out before VIP day ended, with large canvases priced at $125,000 and smaller works at $25,000.

#### THE KOREAN MARKET MOMENT

Tina Kim Gallery's performance deserves its own paragraph. The gallery placed more than $800,000 worth of art during the VIP preview alone: two Ha Chong-Hyun paintings ($180,000 and $250,000), two Lee ShinJa textile works ($70,000 and $180,000), a Kim Tschang-Yeul painting ($70,000), two Maia Ruth Lee paintings ($25,000 and $18,000), and a Jane Yang D'Haene ceramic work ($25,000).

What Tina Kim has built over years — positioning Korean modernism not as a regional curiosity but as a foundational chapter in postwar abstraction — is paying off consistently. Frieze LA remains the most receptive market for it.

#### FOCUS: THE EMERGING MARKET'S STRONGEST CASE

The Focus section, curated for the third consecutive year by Essence Harden, may be the fair's most quietly consequential corner. Supported by Stone Island, it brings together 15 US-based galleries founded within the past twelve years, each presenting a single artist at depth.

The results were immediate and institutional.

Sea View gallery's solo presentation of recent UCLA graduate Zenobia Lee sold out during VIP preview, with works priced between $7,000 and $20,000. Two pieces were acquired by institutions: one by the California African American Museum (CAAM), another by the Mohn Art Collective (MAC3) — the joint acquisition fund operated by the Hammer Museum, LACMA, and MOCA. Make Room gallery's presentation of Erica Mahinay also sold out in full (ten paintings, $5,500–$35,000), with one work selected by the Santa Monica Art Bank. The 2026 Frieze Impact Prize winner Napoles Marty's booth sold out entirely.

The gatekeeping function of the traditional gallery career ladder is being compressed. For advisors watching the market, the Focus section is not supplementary programming — it is a primary signal generator.

#### INSTITUTIONAL ACQUISITIONS: THE COMPLETE PICTURE

Mohn Art Collective / MAC3 (Hammer + LACMA + MOCA): Clarissa Tossin, LA Weeds: First Harvest (2026), from kaufmann repetto; Zenobia Lee, from Sea View; Sharif Farrag, from Jeffrey Deitch.

California African American Museum Acquisition Fund: Zenobia Lee, from Sea View; Jessica Taylor Bellamy, Linear Burn (2025), from Anat Ebgi ($11,000).

Santa Monica Art Bank × Frieze LA Acquisition Fund: Erica Mahinay, Unfetter (Blue Gaze) (2025), from Make Room.

Five works. Five institutions. All artists at the beginning of their careers. All placed into permanent public collections as a direct result of one fair. That is by design, and it is working.

#### BEYOND THE BOOTH

Frieze Projects (in partnership with Art Production Fund, under the banner Body & Soul) brought new commissions to the Santa Monica Airport campus. Amanda Ross-Ho's durational performance Untitled Orbit (MANUAL MODE) ran throughout the week. Cosmas & Damian Brown's Fountain: Sources of Light (2026) incorporated metal plates and bowls painted in workshops with local children. Shana Hoehn's outdoor sculpture Deadfall (2026) — a city tree transformed into cheerleader legs via the Santa Monica Urban Forest program — became one of the fair's most-photographed works.

The Frieze Library, launched this year in direct response to the Palisades fire, invited participating galleries to donate art publications to the newly reopened Pacific Palisades Library. A small gesture, and precisely the right one.

The Deutsche Bank Frieze Los Angeles Film Award ($10,000) went to Joey Bueno Breese for El Rio Nuestro, with Devin O'Guinn taking the Audience Award.

#### THE ARI EMANUEL MOMENT

No account of Frieze LA 2026 is complete without the story of the quilts.

Ari Emanuel — CEO of Mari Group, which acquired Frieze last year — arrived at the Santa Monica Airport campus before the VIP doors opened on February 26. He walked directly to the booth of Fort Gansevoort, a New York gallery making its Frieze LA debut, presenting a solo show by Yvonne Wells, an 86-year-old quiltmaker from Tuscaloosa, Alabama. Within three minutes, he committed to three works: Wells's quilted portraits of Michael Jackson, Marilyn Monroe, and Elvis Presley, priced between $50,000 and $60,000 each.

"He said, 'These are amazing,'" Fort Gansevoort's founder Adam Shopkorn recounted the following day. "His wife came alongside him. Within three minutes he told me, 'I am going to take Michael Jackson, Marilyn Monroe, and Elvis Presley.' I said, 'That's amazing. Thank you so much.'" The transaction was finalized through Emanuel's art advisor, Jeffrey Deitch, whose gallery was also exhibiting at the fair. It was the fair's first sale. It was also its most human story.

#### FELIX ART FAIR: A DIFFERENT KIND OF SERIOUS

Six blocks and a world away — at the Hollywood Roosevelt Hotel — Felix Art Fair was doing what it has always done: running a fundamentally different kind of fair.

Now in its eighth edition, Felix was founded in 2018 by collector Dean Valentine alongside dealers Al and Mills Morán. The premise has never changed: 57 galleries taking over hotel rooms, suites, and poolside cabanas around a David Hockney-painted pool, conducting business at a pace that feels less like a trade fair and more like a very well-curated gathering with uncommonly good art.

Warmer-than-expected late February weather helped. For blizzard-weary visitors flying in from New York, the Roosevelt's sun-drenched pool deck was a genuine relief. Social energy translated into sales energy.

#### FELIX: STANDOUT SALES AND WORK

The fair's most-discussed early transaction was Stroll Garden's presentation of Raina Lee — stoneware works referencing Monet, Van Gogh, and Sargent, priced at $6,000 to $8,000 each. Eleven of twelve pieces sold before the Wednesday preview closed. The twelfth was on hold.

The work that generated the most sustained critical attention was Maddy Inez's ceramic fountain The Sower (2023), presented in Megan Mulrooney's cabana. Inez is the daughter of Alison Saar and granddaughter of Betye Saar — a lineage felt in the work without overwhelming it. The sound of trickling water gave the space a meditative quality that suited the setting exactly.

Felix also launched The Felix Podcast this year, hosted by Dean Valentine and journalist Janelle Zara, with inaugural guests including philanthropist Jarl Mohn and artists Frances Stark and Joey Terrill.

#### THE SATELLITE PICTURE

LA Art Week 2026 extended well beyond its two anchor fairs.

Butter Art Fair (Inglewood) — Co-founder Mali Bacon describes the fair, now making its LA debut, as a correction to the 2020 moment when institutional attention to Black art too often centered the work without the artists. Its public, portfolio-based selection process and emphasis on artist welfare gave it a distinct ethos.

Enzo Art Fair (Echo Park) — A new free-admission fair organized by collector R. Parmar, offering New York and international galleries a low-risk LA entry point in a hybrid forum-fair format.

Startup Art Fair (Venice Beach, The Kinney) — Returning after five years away, the artist-run hotel-room format ran a shuttle to Frieze.

Post-Fair (former Santa Monica Post Office) — Now in its second year under gallerist Chris Sharp, Post-Fair has quickly established itself as the fair week's thoughtful, unhurried coda. One-third local, two-thirds international, consistently showing more experimental work with more generous floor space than a booth allows.

Julia Stoschek Foundation (Variety Arts Theater, Downtown LA) — Not a fair, but one of the week's most significant events: the Foundation's first US exhibition, an immersive time-based media survey with works by Marina Abramović, Arthur Jafa, Anne Imhof, and Jordan Wolfson, among others, occupying six floors of a historic downtown theater.

#### WHAT 2026 LEAVES BEHIND:

Three things from this edition will matter beyond the week.

The institutional pipeline is working. The coordinated acquisition infrastructure — MAC3, CAAM, the Santa Monica Art Bank — is putting emerging work directly into permanent public collections off the fair floor. It creates provenance. It creates price floors. And it signals that LA institutions are active participants in the market, not passive observers.

The emerging market is being validated at speed. Zenobia Lee, Erica Mahinay, Napoles Marty — none of these artists spent years building gallery careers before reaching a platform like Frieze. They arrived early and left with institutional stamps of approval. That speed of validation is accelerating, and it changes how advisors and collectors should think about the Focus section specifically.

Los Angeles is not trying to be New York. The most important thing Frieze LA has done over seven editions is resist the temptation to replicate Frieze New York's scale and density, building instead something that works specifically here: intimate enough to feel like a community event, large enough to matter commercially, rooted enough in local culture to feel necessary rather than transplanted. Felix amplifies that quality. The new satellite fairs are beginning to follow.

Santa Monica Airport — the fair's home since 2023 — is scheduled to close in 2028. This was one of the last two editions to use it. Whatever replaces it will inherit a fair that has figured out what it wants to be. In a city that has been through a great deal recently, that is worth noting.

signal.title: NEW MEDIA ART-FOCUSED SEO-SEOUL MUSEUM OF ART TO OPEN
signal.body:
The Seo-Seoul Museum of Art, Seoul’s first public museum dedicated to new media art, is set to open in Geumcheon-gu on March 12.

The museum will operate exhibitions and research programs focused on new media, experimenting with emerging artistic mediums and languages. It will also emphasize arts education to nurture youth and future creative talents.

In March, the opening exhibitions will include the Sema performance 《Breath》, the construction record exhibition 《Our Time Begins Here》, and the new media collection exhibition 《Transparent Seo-Seoul |Youth| Machine》. In August, the museum will host the media artist exhibition 《Heecheon Kim: The Moles》.

Alongside exhibitions, the Seo-Seoul Museum of Art will launch a long-term research project titled “Korean Media Art Studies” and annually publish the “Media Artist Research Series”, which provides in-depth studies of Korean media artists. The museum will also publish its research series Performance and Museum.

---

Recently, Korean new media artists have been actively working both domestically and internationally, drawing global attention.

1. Ayoung Kim — Global Media Art Leader

- Artistic Focus: Constructs virtual environments and storytelling by integrating advanced technologies such as virtual reality, motion capture, game engines, and AI with human experience.

- Key Awards/Exhibitions: Selected as the 2025 LG Guggenheim Award recipient—the first Korean to receive this prestigious award recognizing innovation in tech-based art. Held a solo exhibition at Hamburger Bahnhof, Berlin (2025) and exhibited at MoMA PS1, New York (2025).

- Collections: Works held in international collections including Frac Lorraine, Leeum Museum of Art, and Tate London.

2. Heecheon Kim — Rising Global Artist

- Artistic Focus: Experiments with the intersection of reality and virtuality through digital technologies such as GPS, AR, and VR, exploring identity and perception.

- International Recognition: Named one of Artnet News’ “7 Asian Artists to Watch,” recognized for integrating digital technology and media into contemporary artistic practice.

- Global Activities: Participated in Istanbul Biennale, Gwangju Biennale, and Seoul Mediacity Biennale, collaborating with institutions including London’s Hayward Gallery.

- Upcoming Exhibitions: Participates in the 2026 group exhibition 「K‑Now! Korean Video Art Today」 at MASI Lugano.

3. YoungEun Kim — ACC Future Prize Winner

- Artistic Focus: Reinterprets historical and social narratives through auditory media in combination with sound, video, and installation.

- Awards: 2026 ACC Future Prize recipient, awarded by the Asia Culture Center (ACC) to artists expanding future art discourse, providing international support and exhibition opportunities. In addition, she received the 2025 Korea Artist Prize from the National Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art (MMCA), further establishing her prominence in the Korean contemporary art scene.

- Upcoming Exhibitions: Large-scale solo exhibition at ACC from August 2026 to January 2027.

4. Young‑Hae Chang Heavy Industries — Web Art Group

- Artistic Focus: Utilizes internet as an artistic medium, primarily through text-based animation, translating Korean experiences and digital culture into a global context.

- Collections/Exhibitions: Works held by Tate Museum, Centre Pompidou, QAGOMA, and M+ Hong Kong.

5. Sung Hwan Kim — Multi-layered Media Installation & Video Artist

- Artistic Focus: Develops complex media installations integrating video, installation, performance, sound, and drawing. Expands both technology-based media and traditional visual languages.

- International Activity: Solo exhibitions in collaboration with Van Abbemuseum (Eindhoven) and ZKM | Center for Art and Media Karlsruhe; participated in Hawaiʻi Triennial (2025), MoMA New York (2021), Gwangju Biennale (2021), and the 57th Venice Biennale (2017).

- Upcoming Exhibitions: Showcasing new works in ZKM collaborative exhibition “Manifesto of Spring” and ACC/Gwangju in 2026; will participate in Bergen Kunsthall (Norway) tour exhibition.

---

#### WHAT MAKES KOREAN NEW MEDIA ART STAND OUT

1. Historical and Cultural Foundations

Following the 1997 IMF crisis, Korea underwent rapid industrialization, digital infrastructure expansion, and exposure to global information networks. Artists and educators emerging during this period grew up in an experimental environment combining technology and art.
Leading Korean art universities (Seoul National University, Hongik University, Korea National University of Arts) actively integrated traditional painting/sculpture education with media art labs and global residency programs, allowing artists to simultaneously develop technology-based experimentation and self-directed creative practice.
Rapid social and cultural changes—including platform labor, metaverse experiences, and digital culture—naturally inform both themes and media in their works.

2. Technological Adoption and Experimental Creativity

Korean artists creatively integrate AI, AR/VR, game engines, sensor technologies, and other digital tools into their artistic language. These technological experiments go beyond novelty, combining social and identity experiences to generate unique creative outputs.
For example, Ayoung Kim’s Delivery Dancer project explores platform labor and human-machine relationships, simultaneously implementing technological experimentation and social themes.

3. Social & Identity Experience and Sensitivity to Global Discourse

Artists experience both homogeneity and difference due to Korea’s rapid industrialization, democratization, and globalization, which is reflected in their work. The domestic art ecosystem actively interacts with tradition, modernity, locality, and globality, enabling Korean artists to establish intercultural resonance in global discourse.
For example, Young-Hae Chang Heavy Industries translates Korean experiences into global codes through internet art, engaging international audiences.

4. Strategic Competitiveness in the Global Art Scene

With increasing attention on Asian emerging artists, Korean artists are recognized as reliable global partners due to their high level of education and technical expertise. Young artists’ proficiency with digital media and social platforms enables efficient project execution in international collaborations.
For example, Ayoung Kim’s Delivery Dancer project, an interactive performance using AI, AR, and sensor technologies, has drawn attention from institutions in New York, London, and Berlin.

In conclusion, the international recognition of Korean new media artists results from a complex combination of technological literacy, experimental creativity, social and identity experiences, domestic support structures, and global art networks.

- Technology-based experimentation + self-directed creation + social/identity experience → unique artistic vision

- Institutional support + global networks → accelerated international presence

- Digital media proficiency → competitive advantage in global collaboration

Korean artists go beyond mere achievements, creating new artistic paradigms, and are expected to continue making increasingly original and influential contributions to the international art scene.

---

#### 서울시립 서서울미술관 개관 & 한국 뉴미디어 아트의 세계적 도약

서울시 최초 공공 뉴미디어 특화 미술관인 서서울미술관이 서울시 금천구에 3월 12일 개관한다.

서서울미술관은 새로운 매체와 언어를 실험하는 뉴미디어 관련 전시와 연구 프로그램을 운영하며, 청소년 및 미래 예술 인재를 양성하는 예술 교육을 중점적으로 진행한다.

올 3월, 개관 특별전으로 세마 퍼포먼스 《호흡》, 건립 기록전 《우리의 시간은 여기서부터》, 뉴미디어 소장품전 《서서울의 투명한 |청소년| 기계》를 개최한다. 오는 8월에는 미디어 작가전 《김희천: 두더지들》을 진행한다.

전시와 더불어, 서서울미술관은 중장기 연구 프로젝트“한국미디어아트 연구”를 시작하며, 한국 미디어 아트 작가를 심층 연구하는“미디어 작가 연구 총서”를 연례로 발간한다. 또한, 미술관 연구 총서『퍼포먼스와 미술관』을 출판한다.

---

최근 한국 출신 뉴미디어 작가들이 국내외에서 활발히 활동하며 글로벌 미술계에서 주목받고 있다.

1. 김아영 (Ayoung Kim) — 글로벌미디어아트선도자

- 작품특징: 가상 현실, 모션 캡처, 게임 엔진, AI 등 첨단 기술과 인간 경험을 결합한 가상 환경 구축과 스토리텔링을 탐구한다.

- 주요수상/전시: 2025년 LG Guggenheim Award 수상자로 선정되었다. Guggenheim Museum과 LG가 기술 기반 예술에 혁신적 기여를 한 작가에게 수여하는 대표 상이며, 한국인으로는 최초 수상자이다. 베를린 Hamburger Bahnhof (2025), MoMA PS1 뉴욕(2025) 등 주요 기관에서 개인전을 했다.

- 주요 소장처: 프락 아르코니아(Frac Lorraine), 리움 미술관, 테이트(Tate, London) 등 국제 컬렉션에 소장

2. 김희천 (Kim Heecheon) — 차세대글로벌라이징아티스트

- 작품특징: GPS, AR, VR 등 디지털 기술을 기반으로 현실과 가상을 융합하며, 정체성과 인식의 교차 지점을 실험한다.

- 국제평가: Artnet News가 선정한 “주목해야 할 떠오르는 아시안 작가 7인” 중 한 명으로 소개되었다. 디지털 기술과 미디어를 적극적으로 작품에 반영하는 동시대 실천이 글로벌 담론과 맞닿는 사례로 평가된다.

- 글로벌행보: 이스탄불 비엔날레, 광주비엔날레, 서울미디어시티비엔날레 등 국제 행사에 참여하였으며, 런던 헤이워드 갤러리 등 주요 기관과 작업을 진행했다.

- 예정전시: 2026년 MASI Lugano의 그룹전 「K‑Now! Korean Video Art Today」에 참여할 예정이다.

3. 김영은 (YoungEun Kim)— ACC Future Prize 수상자

- 작품특징: 사운드, 비디오, 설치를 결합한 다중 매체적 실천을 통해 역사적·사회적 내러티브를 청각적 매체로 재해석한다.

- 주요 수상: 2026년 ACC Future Prize 수상자로 선정되었다. 국립아시아문화전당(ACC)이 미래 예술 담론을 확장하는 작가에게 수여하는 상으로, 국제적 지원과 전시 기회를 제공한다. 또한 2025년에는 국립현대미술관(MMCA) ‘올해의 작가상’을 수상하며 한국 현대미술계에서의 위상을 확고히 했다.

- 예정전시: ACC에서 2026년 8월부터 2027년 1월까지 대규모 개인전을 진행할 예정이다.

4. 장영혜중공업 (Young‑Hae Chang Heavy Industries) — 웹아트그룹

- 작품특징: 텍스트 기반 애니메이션을 중심으로 인터넷이라는 매체 자체를 예술 언어로 활용한다. 한국적 경험과 디지털 문화의 교차를 글로벌 코드로 번역하는 방식이 특징이다.

- 위상: 웹 아트 역사에서 영향력 있는 그룹으로 평가받는다.

- 소장/전시: Tate Museum, Centre Pompidou, QAGOMA, M+ Hong Kong 등 주요 기관 컬렉션에 작품이 소장되어 있다.

5. 김성환 (Sung Hwan Kim) — 다층적미디어설치·영상아티스트

- 작품특징: 비디오, 설치, 퍼포먼스, 사운드, 드로잉 등을 융합한 복합 미디어 설치 작업을 전개한다. 기술 기반 매체뿐 아니라 전통적 영상·설치 언어를 확장한다.

- 국제활동: Van Abbemuseum(에인트호벤)와 함께한 유럽 대규모 개인전과 ZKM | Center for Art and Media Karlsruhe 전시를 개최하였다. Hawaiʻi Triennial(2025), MoMA 뉴욕(2021), 광주비엔날레(2021), 57회 베니스비엔날레(2017) 등 국제 미술 이벤트에도 참여하였다.

- 예정전시: ZKM 협력전 ‘Manifesto of Spring’ 및 ACC/Gwangju 전시 파트로 2026년 중 새로운 작품을 선보일 예정이다. Bergen Kunsthall(노르웨이) 순회전에도 참여할 계획이다.

---

#### 세계가이들을주목하는이유

1. 역사·문화적 배경과 창작 토대

1990년대 후반 IMF 이후 한국 사회는 급속한 산업화, 디지털 인프라 확장, 글로벌 정보 네트워크 환경에 노출되었다. 이 시기 등장한 예술 교육자와 작가 세대는 기술과 예술을 결합한 실험적 환경에서 성장하였다.
국내 주요 미술대학(서울대, 홍익대, 한예종 등)은 전통 회화·조각 교육뿐 아니라 미디어 아트·뉴미디어 실험실과 글로벌 레지던시 연계를 활발히 진행하여, 작가들이 기술 기반 실험과 자기주도적 창작 경험을 동시에 쌓을 수 있는 구조를 갖추었다.
급격한 사회·문화 변화 경험(플랫폼 노동, 메타버스, 디지털 문화 등)은 작품 속 주제와 매체로 자연스럽게 반영된다.

2. 기술적 수용과 창작 실험정신

한국 작가들은 AI, AR/VR, 게임 엔진, 센서 기술 등 최신 디지털 기술을 예술적 언어로 창의적으로 융합한다. 이러한 기술적 실험은 단순한 신기술 활용이 아니라, 사회적·정체성적 경험과 결합된 창작력으로 이어진다.
예를 들어 김아영의 ‘Delivery Dancer’ 프로젝트는 플랫폼 노동과 인간/기계 관계를 탐구하며, 기술적 실험과 사회적 주제를 동시에 구현한다.

3. 사회적·정체성 경험과 글로벌담론 민감성

한국 사회의 독특한 경험(급속한 산업화·민주화·글로벌화)으로 인해 작가들은 동질성과 차별성을 동시에 경험하며 이를 작품에 녹여낸다. 국내 미술 생태계는 전통과 현대, 지역성과 세계성 사이의 상호작용이 활발하여, 한국 작가들은 글로벌 담론 속에서도 상호문화적 공감대를 형성할 수 있다.
예를 들어 Young-Hae Chang Heavy Industries는 인터넷 아트를 통해 한국적 경험을 글로벌 코드로 번역하고 국제 관객과 소통한다.

4. 글로벌 미술계에서의 전략적 경쟁력

아시아 신진 작가에 대한 기관·컬렉터의 관심이 증가하는 상황에서, 한국 작가는 교육·기술 수준이 높아 신뢰할 수 있는 글로벌 파트너로 인식된다. 젊은 작가는 디지털 매체·소셜 플랫폼 활용 능력이 뛰어나 해외 기관과 협업에서 빠르고 효과적인 프로젝트 실행이 가능하다.
예를 들어 김아영의 ‘Delivery Dancer’ 프로젝트는 AI·AR·센서 기술을 활용한 인터랙티브 퍼포먼스로 뉴욕, 런던, 베를린 주요 기관에서 주목받았다.

결론적으로, 한국 출신 뉴미디어 작가들이 국제적으로 각광받는 이유는 단순한 기술 친화력이나 창의적 실험을 넘어서, 한국 사회·문화·교육구조와 글로벌 미술계 환경이 맞물린 복합적 결과이다.

- 기술 기반 실험 + 자기주도적 창작 + 사회·정체성 경험 → 독창적 작품력

- 국내 제도적 지원 + 글로벌 네트워크 → 국제 진출 가속화

- 디지털 매체 활용 능력 → 글로벌 협업에서 경쟁력 확보

한국 작가들은 단순한 성과를 넘어 새로운 예술적 패러다임을 만들어내며, 앞으로 국제 미술계에서 더욱 독창적이고 영향력 있는 행보를 이어갈 것으로 기대된다.

signal.title: RM X SFMOMA IN 2026
signal.body:
## KOREAN ART MEET THE WORLD: RM X SFMOMA

The San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (SFMOMA) has announced a groundbreaking exhibition, RM x SFMOMA, the first museum presentation to feature artworks from the personal collection of RM (Kim Namjoon) — the leader of global pop phenomenon BTS — curated by RM himself in collaboration with América Castillo and Hyoeun Kim of SFMOMA. The exhibition will run from October 2026 to February 2027, marking a historic moment as the museum’s first collaboration with a K-pop artist.

Chusa Kim Jeong-hui, Orchid Paintings and Writings, 19th century, Joseon dynasty; Collection of RM

Bringing together 200 works from RM’s private collection and SFMOMA’s holdings, RM x SFMOMA invites viewers to explore the interplay between Korean modernism and international contemporary art. Many of these works will be on public view for the first time, offering a rare glimpse into RM’s deeply personal yet globally attuned collecting vision.

“We live in an age defined by boundaries,” RM said. “This exhibition reflects those boundaries — between East and West, Korea and America, the modern and the contemporary, the personal and the universal. My only hope is that this exhibition can be a small but sturdy bridge for many.”

RM’s carefully assembled collection spans the work of key figures in Korean modern and contemporary art — including Yun Hyong-keun, Park Rehyun, Kwon Okyon, Kim Yun Shin, To Sangbong, and Chang Ucchin — alongside pieces by major Western artists such as Mark Rothko, Agnes Martin, Henri Matisse, Georgia O’Keeffe,and Paul Klee.

Yun Hyong-keun, Blue-Umber ’79-C6 (1979). Collection of RM; © Yun Seong-ryeol, courtesy PKM Gallery

At SFMOMA, these works will enter into dialogue with highlights from the museum’s own collection, including Kim Whanki’s luminous abstract paintings and modern masterpieces by Philip Guston, Giorgio Morandi, and Roni Horn, creating a cross-cultural conversation between artistic languages and histories.

“Visitors will have an unprecedented opportunity to explore RM’s beautiful and contemplative collection in dialogue with SFMOMA’s holdings,” said Janet Bishop, Thomas Weisel Family Chief Curator at SFMOMA. “It’s an invitation to make new discoveries and reflect on our own relationships with art.”

Kim Whanki, 26-I-70 (1970). San Francisco Museum of Modern Art, gift of Mrs. Whanki Kim; © Estate of Whanki Kim. Photo: Katherine Du Tiel

RM’s journey as a collector began in 2018 after a transformative visit to the Art Institute of Chicago, which sparked a deep and enduring passion for visual art. Since then, he has become an influential voice in Korea’s contemporary art scene, often referencing artists in his lyrics, album artwork, and interviews. His 2022 solo album Indigo paid tribute to painter Yun Hyong-keun, further demonstrating the profound link between his music and visual art.

Recognized for his thoughtful engagement with both sound and image, RM’s collaboration with SFMOMA represents a new chapter in his creative evolution — one that bridges pop culture and the global art world.

RM x SFMOMA is organized by the San Francisco Museum of Modern Art in partnership with BIGHIT MUSIC, HYBE, and HYBE INSIGHT.

### -
ABOUT SFMOMA

The San Francisco Museum of Modern Art is one of the largest museums of modern and contemporary art in the United States. Its LEED Gold-certified building, designed by Snøhetta and Mario Botta, houses seven gallery floors and over 45,000 square feet of free, art-filled public space, making it a vibrant cultural hub for the Bay Area.

More:

https://www.sfmoma.org/press-release/rm-and-sfmoma-partner-on-first-exhibition-of-its-kind/

signal.title: ON VIEW: EXHIBITION HIGHLIGHTS
signal.body:
## SEOUL ART NOW - EXHIBITION HIGHLIGHTS

Mark Bradford — ‘Keep Walking’

Amorepacific Museum of Art, Seoul
August 7, 2025 – January 4, 2026

Bradford’s large-scale survey unfolds as a dense topography of social memory. Layered canvases, paper fragments, and painted interventions map fractured histories of race, migration, and violence. The exhibition is structured less as chronology than as rhythm—oscillating between monumental canvases and taut, intimate works—forcing viewers to navigate a field where abstraction becomes political ground.

Antony Gormley

‘Drawing on Space’
Museum SAN, Wonju
June 20 – November 30, 2025

Set against Tadao Ando’s subterranean concrete dome, this exhibition unfolds across 48 works spanning sculpture, drawing, and installation. The centerpiece, Ground, conceived with Ando, anchors the show as a permanent spatial intervention. Suspended forms and cast figures probe the thresholds between body, void, and built environment.

‘Inextricable’
White Cube Seoul & Thaddaeus Ropac, Seoul
September 2 – October 18, 2025

The dual-gallery project splits Gormley’s practice into two registers. White Cube stages dense sculptural encounters with Blockworks and Bunker forms pressing against space, while Ropac offers the tensile geometries of Extended Strapworks and Open Blockworks. Together they articulate how the human body is now shaped, even disciplined, by architectural logic.

Lee Bul — ‘From 1998 to Now’

Leeum, Samsung Museum of Art, Seoul
September 4, 2025 – January 4, 2026

Leeum dedicates its galleries to a sweeping retrospective of Lee Bul’s 27-year practice. From cyborg sculptures and mutant hybrids of the late 1990s to immersive mirrored environments of the present, the show underscores her ability to stage rupture as spectacle. The curation unfolds as a play of surfaces—reflective, broken, kaleidoscopic—where history collides with futurity.

Louise Bourgeois — ‘The Evanescent and the Eternal’

Hoam Museum of Art, Seoul
August 30, 2025 – January 4, 2026

The long-awaited Bourgeois retrospective in Korea presents a deeply resonant cross-section of sculpture, drawings, and textile works. Curated around states of fragility and endurance, the exhibition dramatizes her lifelong dialogue with memory, trauma, and architectural form. The galleries oscillate between intimate enclosures and monumental presences, staging a psychological theatre in space.

Kim Tschang-Yeul — ‘A Retrospective’

National Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art, Seoul
August 22 – December 21, 2025

The Seoul museum gathers Kim’s iconic water-drop paintings alongside rarely seen abstractions, framing them as meditations on time and ephemerality. Repetition becomes its own image: light suspended on canvas, the instant crystallized. The retrospective captures Kim not only as a painter of motifs but as a philosopher of perception.

MMCA Collection — ‘Korean Modern & Contemporary Art’

National Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art, Gwacheon
May 01 -  June 27, 2027

The new collection display maps a century of Korean art from modernist beginnings to the contemporary scene. Dansaekhwa anchors the narrative, with Park Seo-Bo, Ha Chong-Hyun, and Chung Sang-Hwa placed in dialogue with younger conceptualists. Rather than a static timeline, the show reads as a living archive, revisited and rewritten by each generation.

Hilma af Klint – ‘Proper Summons’

Busan Museum of Contemporary Art, Gallery 5 (2F)
July 19 – October 26, 2025

The exhibition brings together over 140 paintings, drawings, videos, and archival materials spanning Hilma af Klint’s spiritual-abstract vision. It is her first major retrospective in Korea, following a run in Tokyo, and it underscores how her work—which she held in private for decades—now speaks broadly to questions of form, mysticism, and perception.

Gala Porras-Kim — ‘The Unseen Record’
Kukje Gallery, Seoul
September 4 – October 26, 2025

The Los Angeles–based korean artist’s first major solo exhibition in Korea stages a conceptual inquiry into how artifacts, languages, and cultural memory are preserved—or misinterpreted—by institutions. Porras-Kim’s drawings, sculptures, and sound pieces probe the boundaries of museological authority, repositioning the act of conservation as a site of creative speculation.

Kim Soo-ja— ‘Breath’

Sunhyewon (SK Foundation Heritage Space), Seoul

September 3 – October 19, 2025

Hosted in the restored Sunhyewon compound, the mirrored floor work transforms the floor plane into a portal, reflecting rafters, rooflines, and the viewer’s feet so that above and below merge. Accompanied by four other works, the exhibition extends Kim’s long meditation on memory, identity, and the body as archive.

Lee Kang-So — ‘Flowing Water, Ongoing Experiment’

Daegu Art Museum, Daegu

September 9, 2025 – February 22, 2026

This sweeping retrospective charts Lee Kang-So’s five-decade engagement with experimental art, traversing painting, sculpture, installation, prints, drawings, and archival works. It pivots around two conceptual axes: 'gok-su-ji-yu' (the tradition of letting a cup drift on flowing water as spontaneous poetic practice) and the ethos of sustained experimentation.

signal.title: ASIA’S ART MARKET - FIRST HALF OF 2025
signal.body:
The first half of 2025 has revealed a highly dynamic and contradictory landscape across Asia’s art market—where new energy from emerging collectors meets persistent structural challenges. As the region becomes an increasingly central player in the global art ecosystem, signals of optimism are emerging, albeit tempered by macroeconomic uncertainties and the aftershocks of a prolonged market slowdown.

A Youthful Shift: The Rise of Under-30 Collectors

Perhaps the most noteworthy development this year is the visible rise of young collectors—particularly those under 30—actively shaping the future of the Asian art market. These collectors, hailing not only from mainland China, Taiwan, and Korea but also from Asian diasporas based in Europe, were observed attending major art events in Zurich and Basel for the first time. Their presence represents more than a generational change—it signals a fresh wave of cultural interest, technological fluency, and global engagement that could redefine long-term regional growth.

Hong Kong’s Repositioning and the Auction House Pivot

Hong Kong continues to redefine its role within the global market, despite ongoing geopolitical and financial pressures. This March, Christie’s, Sotheby’s, Phillips, and Bonhams aligned their major auctions with Art Basel Hong Kong, reinforcing the city's strategic position in the international auction calendar. While the broader Chinese market remains in a state of contraction, Hong Kong’s infrastructure and cross-border appeal offer it a unique edge in the region’s evolving market architecture.

South & West Asia Gain Traction

South Asian art is experiencing unprecedented momentum. From record-breaking auction results to increased institutional visibility, artists from India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka are garnering wider international recognition. In parallel, West Asia is undergoing its own transformation: Sotheby’s held its first-ever auction in Saudi Arabia, Art Basel is preparing a new fair in Doha, and Art Dubai has expanded its leadership and programming. These developments speak to a more diverse and decentralized definition of “Asia” in the global art world.

Cross-Continental Collaborations and Artist Breakthroughs

The push for internationalization continues, with Asian galleries strengthening their presence abroad. Hong Kong’s Kiang Malingue has opened a space in New York, while Seoul’s Gallery Kiwa now operates in London. Korean artist Rim Park, through Seoul-based Cylinder Gallery, is now represented by Kraupa-Tuskany Zeidler in Berlin. Anna Park has become the youngest artist represented by Lehmann Maupin.

Meanwhile, Japanese painter Yu Nishimura gained attention with his first U.S. solo show at David Zwirner and participation in Art Basel’s Unlimited section. Hong Kong-born Kasing Lung’s character “Labubu” has become a global pop phenomenon, reaching beyond the traditional art world.

Market Contraction and Political Headwinds in Korea

Despite positive signals, Korea has faced unique headwinds. Several global galleries that entered the Seoul market between 2021 and 2023 are now scaling back or suspending operations. The key reasons include currency volatility, inflationary pressure, and political instability. When Thaddaeus Ropac opened in Seoul in 2021, the USD-KRW rate was below 1,250. In 2025, it has remained above 1,400, increasing local collectors’ cost burden by up to 20%.

Tensions escalated in December 2024 with the declaration of martial law and the president’s impeachment. As a result, institutions temporarily closed or delayed programming due to safety concerns. The launch of a new art fair, Selection Seoul, was postponed to 2026 due to weak currency and sluggish sentiment.

These developments have raised concerns over Seoul’s position as a global art hub. The withdrawal of foreign galleries risks weakening Asia’s international connectivity. Political and economic stability will be critical for any long-term recovery.

Conclusion: A Market in Transition, Poised for a Pivotal Fall Season

Asia’s art market stands at a strategic crossroads—supported by generational renewal, regional diversification, and a growing global presence, yet challenged by volatility and uneven infrastructure. Upcoming events such as Frieze Seoul, Kiaf Seoul, and Tokyo Gendai will be critical in testing the market’s resilience and ability to bounce back.

The second half of 2025 may not resolve all issues, but it will likely define the pace and direction of Asia’s next chapter in the global art economy.

KR

2025 아시아 미술시장: 새로운 세대의 부상과 구조적 성장통

2025년 상반기 아시아 미술시장은 빠르게 변화하는 흐름 속에서 상반된 조짐을 동시에 보여주고 있다. 젊은 컬렉터층의 등장, 경매사의 아시아 집중, 남·서아시아 미술의 약진 등 긍정적인 변화가 감지되는 반면, 정치적 불안과 시장 침체, 외국 갤러리들의 철수 등 부정적인 요인도 뚜렷하게 나타나고 있다.

30대 이하 신진 컬렉터의 부상

올해 아시아 미술시장에서 가장 주목할 변화는 30대 이하 신진 컬렉터들의 활발한 움직임이다. 이들은 중국 본토, 대만, 한국뿐만 아니라 유럽에 거주하는 아시아 출신 컬렉터들로 구성되어 있으며, 바젤과 취리히의 페어 위크에 처음으로 참석하는 모습이 포착되었다. 이는 단순한 세대교체를 넘어, 아시아 미술시장에 새로운 문화적 관심과 글로벌 참여, 그리고 디지털 기반의 접근이 가속화되고 있음을 보여준다.

재편 중인 홍콩의 위상과 경매사 전략

2025년 3월, Christie’s, Sotheby’s, Phillips,  Bonham 등 주요 경매사들이 아트바젤 홍콩 일정에 맞춰 세일을 진행하며, 홍콩은 국제 경매 캘린더 내 입지를 강화했다. 중국 본토 시장이 위축된 가운데, 홍콩은 여전히 아시아 내 거래 플랫폼으로서 차별화된 인프라와 역할을 유지하고 있다.

남아시아와 서아시아 미술의 성장

남아시아 미술은 경매 및 기관 전시에서 사상 최고 수준의 성과를 보이고 있으며, 서아시아 지역도 아트 두바이의 조직 개편, 소더비의 사우디 첫 경매, 아트바젤의 도하 페어 론칭 등으로 주목받고 있다. 아시아 미술시장의 지역 구도가 보다 다원화되고 있는 추세이다.

활발해지는 글로벌 협업과 작가 약진

아시아 갤러리들의 해외 진출도 이어지고 있다. 홍콩의 키앙 말링구는 뉴욕에, 서울의 갤러리 키와는 런던에 지점을 개설했다. 한국 작가 박예림 (Rim Park)은 서울의 실린더 갤러리를 통해 베를린의 크라우파-투스카니 자이들러가 대표하게 되었으며, 안나 박(Anna Park)은 리만 머핀이 대표하는 최연소 작가가 되었다.
일본 작가 니시무라 유는 데이비드 즈위너 뉴욕에서 첫 미국 개인전을 열었으며, 아트바젤 언리미티드 섹션에서도 주목을 받았다. 홍콩 출신의 카싱 렁이 만든 ‘라부부’는 전 세계적으로 컬렉터와 대중문화 시장에서 동시에 인기를 얻으며 경매 기록을 경신하고 있다.

한국 미술시장의 불안정성과 외국 갤러리 철수

긍정적인 흐름에도 불구하고, 한국 미술시장은 독자적인 위기를 겪고 있다. 2021년부터 2023년 사이 서울에 진출한 글로벌 갤러리들 중 다수가 환율 급등, 물가 상승, 정치 불안정성 등으로 인해 운영을 중단하거나 철수를 검토하고 있다. 타데우스 로팍 갤러리가 서울에 진출했을 당시 원-달러 환율은 1,250원 미만이었으나, 현재는 1,400원대를 유지하고 있어 외화 작품 가격이 10~20% 상승한 셈이다.

2024년 12월 계엄령 선포와 대통령 탄핵은 미술시장에 직접적인 충격을 주었고, 문화기관들의 임시 휴관 및 행사 연기 사태로 이어졌다. 특히 2025년 봄 출범 예정이었던 신생 아트페어 ‘셀렉션 서울’은 약세 원화와 위축된 소비심리로 인해 내년으로 연기되었다.
서울은 글로벌 미술허브로서의 입지를 재정립해야 하는 과제에 직면해 있다. 외국 갤러리들의 철수는 아시아 미술시장의 국제적 연결성을 약화시킬 수 있으며, 향후 회복을 위해서는 정치적 안정성과 경제적 신뢰 회복이 선행되어야 한다.

결론: 전환점에 선 아시아, 하반기 시즌이 분수령 될 것

아시아 미술시장은 지금, 구조적 재편과 세대교체가 교차하는 전환점에 서 있다. 프리즈 서울, 키아프 서울, 도쿄 겐다이 등 주요 아트페어가 예정된 2025년 하반기는 시장 회복 가능성을 가늠하는 핵심 시기가 될 것이다.
단순한 회복이 아닌, 정치적 안정, 경제 신뢰 회복, 그리고 신진 컬렉터층과 글로벌 협력 기반이 유기적으로 작동할 수 있는 환경 조성이 무엇보다 중요하다. 앞으로의 선택과 대응이 아시아 미술시장의 다음 장을 결정짓게 될 것이다.

signal.title: RETHINKING ART FAIRS: TAIPEI DANGDAI PAUSES 2026 EDITION
signal.body:
Taipei Dangdai, one of Asia's prominent contemporary art fairs, has officially announced the cancellation of its 2026 edition. The decision, confirmed by parent company Art Assembly, comes as the group undertakes a comprehensive strategic review in response to shifting geopolitical realities and increasing competition within the Asian art fair circuit. Since its launch in 2019, Taipei Dangdai has served as a critical entry point to the East Asian art market, attracting top-tier international galleries and high-profile collectors. However, growing concerns about regional political volatility, currency instability, and a saturated fair landscape have prompted organizers to pause operations beyond the 2025 edition. While not presented as a permanent closure, the cancellation of the 2026 event signals a broader recalibration of fair strategies across the Asia-Pacific region. Industry observers note that Taipei Dangdai’s future will depend heavily on its ability to offer a sustainable value proposition in a landscape increasingly dominated by more established hubs like Hong Kong, Seoul, and Tokyo. From a gallery perspective, the absence of a key regional fair like Taipei Dangdai raises questions about long-term visibility and access to collectors in the Taiwanese market. For collectors, particularly those based in Greater China or Southeast Asia, the move may further concentrate their travel and acquisition efforts around larger events such as Art Basel Hong Kong, Frieze Seoul, or Tokyo Gendai. As global art fair organizers face rising operational costs, political unpredictability, and changing audience behaviors, Taipei Dangdai’s 2026 cancellation reflects a moment of reckoning — not only for the fair itself, but also for the broader model of international fair expansion in Asia. Art Assembly has indicated it will continue to explore alternative models to support the local ecosystem and maintain engagement with the Taiwanese art scene. However, in the absence of a physical fair, stakeholders across the board — galleries, collectors, advisors — will need to reassess their regional strategies heading into the second half of the decade.

KR

타이베이 당다이, 2026년 개최 취소… 아시아 미술시장 재검토 속 전략적 조정

타이베이 당다이(Taipei Dangdai)는 아시아를 대표하는 현대미술 페어 중 하나로, 2026년 행사의 공식 취소를 발표했다. 모회사인 아트 어셈블리(Art Assembly)가 지지하는 이번 결정은 변동하는 지정학적 상황과 아시아 미술 페어 시장 내 경쟁 심화에 대응하기 위한 전략적 전면 재검토 과정에서 내려졌다.

2019년 출범 이래, 타이베이 당다이는 동아시아 미술시장으로 진입하는 핵심 통로 역할을 하며, 최고급 국제 갤러리와 주요 컬렉터들을 끌어모았다. 그러나 지역 정치 불안, 환율 변동성, 그리고 과포화된 페어 시장 환경에 대한 우려가 커지면서, 주최 측은 2025년 이후 운영을 잠정 중단하기로 결정했다.

이번 2026년 행사의 취소는 영구적인 중단으로 보이지 않으나, 아시아 태평양 지역 미술 페어들의 전략적 재조정 신호로 해석된다. 업계 전문가들은 타이베이 당다이의 향후 성패가 홍콩, 서울, 도쿄 등 보다 성숙한 시장과 경쟁 속에서 지속가능한 가치를 제시할 수 있느냐에 달려 있다고 보고 있다.

갤러리 입장에서는 타이베이 당다이라는 주요 지역 페어가 부재함에 따라 대만 시장 내 장기적 가시성과 컬렉터 접근성에 대한 우려가 커지고 있다. 특히 중화권과 동남아시아에 기반을 둔 컬렉터들 입장에서는 아트 바젤 홍콩, 프리즈 서울, 도쿄 겐다이 등 대형 행사에 집중하는 경향이 더욱 강화될 전망이다.

전 세계 미술 페어 주최자들이 운영비 상승, 정치적 불확실성, 변화하는 관객 행태 등에 직면한 가운데, 타이베이 당다이의 2026년 행사 취소는 페어 자체뿐만 아니라 아시아 국제 페어 확장 모델 전반에 대한 근본적인 재검토의 순간임을 보여준다.

아트 어셈블리는 지역 미술 생태계 지원과 대만 미술계와의 지속적인 교류를 위한 대안적 모델 모색을 계속할 예정이라고 밝혔다. 다만 실물 아트페어가 부재한 상황에서, 갤러리, 컬렉터, 아트 어드바이저 등 모든 이해관계자들은 2020년대 후반을 앞두고 지역 전략을 재정비할 필요가 있다.
